---
title: "Wooldridge Introductory Econometrics -- Ch. 10: Time series basics (via StatEcon)"
Authors:
- Jeffrey M. Wooldridge
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    toc-expand: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

Stata examples reproduced from Wooldridge, J. M., *Introductory Econometrics: A
Modern Approach* (Cengage), Chapter 10, *Basic Regression Analysis with Time
Series Data*.

Every example in this chapter is **ordinary least squares** applied to a time
series. StatEcon has no dedicated time-series verbs, but none is needed here:
lags, trends, seasonal dummies and quadratic terms are just columns of the
`DataFrame`, and `stata_regress` reproduces `reg y x` exactly. Where an example
uses a Stata scalar display or a residual regression, the same quantity is
computed directly from the fitted coefficients.

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using StatEcon
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CSV
using Statistics, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra, Printf, Random
using FixedEffectModels, StatsModels, GLM
using FixedEffectModels: Vcov
using CairoMakie
CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png")
using Optim, ForwardDiff
import Distributions
using Distributions: cdf, pdf, quantile, Normal, TDist, FDist, Chisq

# Artifacts this notebook produces go to `output/`.
const OUT = "output"
isdir(OUT) || mkpath(OUT)
outpath(f) = joinpath(OUT, f)

# Pull a single coefficient out of a fitted FixedEffectModels model by name.
bcoef(m, nm) = FixedEffectModels.coef(m)[findfirst(==(nm), FixedEffectModels.coefnames(m))]
```

## Example 10.1: Static Phillips Curve

```{julia}
#=
use "data/phillips.dta", clear
reg inf unem

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
         inf | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
        unem |   .5023782   .2655624     1.89   0.064    -.0300424    1.034799
       _cons |   1.053566   1.547957     0.68   0.499    -2.049901    4.157033
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Number of obs = 56,  F(1,54) = 3.58,  R-squared = 0.0622,  Root MSE = 2.9715
=#
phillips = dataset("phillips")
println("phillips: ", nrow(phillips), " obs, years ",
        minimum(phillips.year), "-", maximum(phillips.year))
m101 = stata_regress(phillips; y = :inf, x = [:unem])
```

**Data-vintage note.** The bundled `phillips.dta` is the *earlier* edition of the
file (49 annual observations, 1948–1996); the published Stata output above is
from a *later* edition (56 observations, 1948–2003). The OLS code is exactly
`reg inf unem` and is correct, but it runs on different data, so the numbers do
**not** match the published output (Julia: `unem` = .4676257, N = 49; Stata:
.5023782, N = 56). This affects every Phillips-curve example (10.1, and 11.5 /
12.1 / 12.5 in later chapters). No numbers were altered to force agreement.

## Example 10.2: Effects of Inflation and Deficits on Interest Rates

```{julia}
#=
use "data/intdef.dta", clear
reg i3 inf def

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
          i3 | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
         inf |   .6058659   .0821348     7.38   0.000     .4411243    .7706074
         def |   .5130579   .1183841     4.33   0.000     .2756095    .7505062
       _cons |   1.733266    .431967     4.01   0.000     .8668497    2.599682
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Number of obs = 56,  F(2,53) = 40.09,  R-squared = 0.6021,  Root MSE = 1.8432
=#
intdef = dataset("intdef")
m102 = stata_regress(intdef; y = :i3, x = [:inf, :def])
```

**Exact match** with Stata (N = 56, all coefficients and SEs to the printed
digits).

## Example 10.3: Puerto Rican Employment and the Minimum Wage

```{julia}
#=
use "data/prminwge.dta", clear
reg lprepop lmincov lusgnp

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
     lprepop | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
     lmincov |  -.1544442   .0649015    -2.38   0.023    -.2862011   -.0226872
      lusgnp |  -.0121888   .0885134    -0.14   0.891    -.1918806     .167503
       _cons |  -1.054423   .7654065    -1.38   0.177     -2.60828    .4994351
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Number of obs = 38,  F(2,35) = 34.04,  R-squared = 0.6605,  Root MSE = .0557
=#
prminwge = dataset("prminwge")
m103 = stata_regress(prminwge; y = :lprepop, x = [:lmincov, :lusgnp])
```

**Exact match** with Stata (N = 38).

## Example 10.4: Effects of Personal Exemption on Fertility Rates

```{julia}
#=
use "data/fertil3.dta", clear
summ pe

    Variable |        Obs        Mean    Std. dev.       Min        Max
          pe |         72    100.4015    65.87563          0     243.83
=#
fertil3 = dataset("fertil3")
stata_summarize(fertil3, [:pe])
```

```{julia}
#=
reg gfr pe ww2 pill

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
         gfr | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
          pe |     .08254   .0296462     2.78   0.007     .0233819    .1416981
         ww2 |   -24.2384   7.458253    -3.25   0.002    -39.12111   -9.355684
        pill |  -31.59403   4.081068    -7.74   0.000    -39.73768   -23.45039
       _cons |   98.68176   3.208129    30.76   0.000     92.28003    105.0835
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Number of obs = 72,  F(3,68) = 20.38,  R-squared = 0.4734,  Root MSE = 14.685
=#
m104a = stata_regress(fertil3; y = :gfr, x = [:pe, :ww2, :pill])
```

```{julia}
#=
reg gfr pe ww2 pill pe_1 pe_2

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
         gfr | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
          pe |   .0726718   .1255331     0.58   0.565    -.1781094     .323453
         ww2 |   -22.1265   10.73197    -2.06   0.043    -43.56608   -.6869196
        pill |  -31.30499   3.981559    -7.86   0.000    -39.25907   -23.35091
        pe_1 |  -.0057796   .1556629    -0.04   0.970     -.316752    .3051929
        pe_2 |   .0338268   .1262574     0.27   0.790    -.2184013     .286055
       _cons |    95.8705   3.281957    29.21   0.000     89.31403     102.427
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Number of obs = 70,  F(5,64) = 12.73,  R-squared = 0.4986,  Root MSE = 14.27
=#
m104b = stata_regress(fertil3; y = :gfr, x = [:pe, :ww2, :pill, :pe_1, :pe_2])
```

`pe_1` and `pe_2` (the two lags of `pe`) are already stored in the dataset, so
Stata drops the first two years and estimates on N = 70.

```{julia}
#=
test pe_1 pe_2
       F(  2,    64) =    0.05
            Prob > F =    0.9480
=#
stata_test(m104b, ["pe_1", "pe_2"])
```

Estimated long-run propensity (LRP):

```{julia}
#=
display _b[pe]+_b[pe_1]+_b[pe_2]
.10071909
=#
lrp = bcoef(m104b, "pe") + bcoef(m104b, "pe_1") + bcoef(m104b, "pe_2")
@printf("LRP = %.8f\n", lrp)
```

Reparameterised regression that puts the LRP and its standard error directly on
the `pe` coefficient (`dif1 = pe_1 - pe`, `dif2 = pe_2 - pe`):

```{julia}
#=
gen dif1=pe_1-pe
gen dif2=pe_2-pe
reg gfr pe dif1 dif2 ww2 pill

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
         gfr | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
          pe |   .1007191   .0298027     3.38   0.001     .0411814    .1602568
        dif1 |  -.0057796   .1556629    -0.04   0.970     -.316752    .3051929
        dif2 |   .0338268   .1262574     0.27   0.790    -.2184013     .286055
         ww2 |   -22.1265   10.73197    -2.06   0.043    -43.56608   -.6869196
        pill |  -31.30499   3.981559    -7.86   0.000    -39.25907   -23.35091
       _cons |    95.8705   3.281957    29.21   0.000     89.31403     102.427
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Number of obs = 70,  F(5,64) = 12.73,  R-squared = 0.4986,  Root MSE = 14.27
=#
df104 = copy(fertil3)
df104.dif1 = df104.pe_1 .- df104.pe
df104.dif2 = df104.pe_2 .- df104.pe
m104c = stata_regress(df104; y = :gfr, x = [:pe, :dif1, :dif2, :ww2, :pill])
```

**Exact match** across all four fits. The `pe` coefficient in the last
regression (.1007191, SE .0298027) is the LRP with its standard error.

## Example 10.5: Antidumping Filings and Chemical Imports

```{julia}
#=
use "data/barium.dta", clear
reg lchnimp lchempi lgas lrtwex befile6 affile6 afdec6

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
     lchnimp | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
     lchempi |   3.117193   .4792021     6.50   0.000     2.168718    4.065668
        lgas |   .1963504   .9066172     0.22   0.829    -1.598099      1.9908
      lrtwex |   .9830183   .4001537     2.46   0.015     .1910022    1.775034
     befile6 |   .0595739   .2609699     0.23   0.820    -.4569585    .5761064
     affile6 |  -.0324064   .2642973    -0.12   0.903    -.5555249     .490712
      afdec6 |   -.565245   .2858352    -1.98   0.050    -1.130993    .0005028
       _cons |    -17.803   21.04537    -0.85   0.399    -59.45769    23.85169
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Number of obs = 131,  F(6,124) = 9.06,  R-squared = 0.3049,  Root MSE = .59735
=#
barium = dataset("barium")
m105 = stata_regress(barium; y = :lchnimp,
                     x = [:lchempi, :lgas, :lrtwex, :befile6, :affile6, :afdec6])
```

Change in Chinese barium exports implied by `afdec6`:

```{julia}
#=
display 100*(exp(_b[afdec6])-1)
-43.177908
=#
@printf("100*(exp(b_afdec6)-1) = %.6f\n", 100 * (exp(bcoef(m105, "afdec6")) - 1))
```

**Exact match** (N = 131).

## Example 10.6: Election Outcomes and Economic Performance

```{julia}
#=
use "data/fair.dta", clear
reg demvote partyWH incum pWHgnews pWHinf if year<1996

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
     demvote | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
     partyWH |  -.0434752    .040459    -1.07   0.300    -.1297114    .0427611
       incum |   .0543902   .0234166     2.32   0.035      .004479    .1043015
    pWHgnews |   .0108466   .0041267     2.63   0.019     .0020508    .0196424
      pWHinf |  -.0077017   .0032567    -2.36   0.032    -.0146432   -.0007602
       _cons |    .481062   .0122631    39.23   0.000     .4549238    .5072002
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Number of obs = 20,  F(4,15) = 7.37,  R-squared = 0.6629,  Root MSE = .04957
=#
fair = dataset("fair")
fair96 = filter(:year => <(1996), fair)
m106 = stata_regress(fair96; y = :demvote, x = [:partyWH, :incum, :pWHgnews, :pWHinf])
```

Predicted Democratic vote share at `partyWH = incum = 1`, `pWHgnews = 3`,
`pWHinf = 3.019`:

```{julia}
#=
display _b[_cons]+_b[partyWH]+_b[incum]+_b[pWHgnews]*3+_b[pWHinf]*3.019
.5012655
=#
pred = bcoef(m106, "(Intercept)") + bcoef(m106, "partyWH") + bcoef(m106, "incum") +
       bcoef(m106, "pWHgnews") * 3 + bcoef(m106, "pWHinf") * 3.019
@printf("predicted demvote = %.7f\n", pred)
```

**Exact match** (N = 20; the `if year<1996` filter is reproduced by `filter`).

## Example 10.7: Housing Investment and Prices

```{julia}
#=
use "data/hseinv.dta", clear
reg linvpc lprice

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      linvpc | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
      lprice |   1.240943   .3824192     3.24   0.002     .4680452    2.013841
       _cons |  -.5502345   .0430266   -12.79   0.000    -.6371945   -.4632746
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Number of obs = 42,  F(1,40) = 10.53,  R-squared = 0.2084,  Root MSE = .15542
=#
hseinv = dataset("hseinv")
m107a = stata_regress(hseinv; y = :linvpc, x = [:lprice])
```

Adding a linear time trend `t` (already in the dataset):

```{julia}
#=
reg linvpc lprice t

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      linvpc | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
      lprice |  -.3809612   .6788352    -0.56   0.578    -1.754035    .9921125
           t |   .0098287   .0035122     2.80   0.008     .0027246    .0169328
       _cons |  -.9130595   .1356133    -6.73   0.000    -1.187363   -.6387557
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Number of obs = 42,  F(2,39) = 10.08,  R-squared = 0.3408,  Root MSE = .14364
=#
m107b = stata_regress(hseinv; y = :linvpc, x = [:lprice, :t])
```

**Exact match.** Controlling for the trend, the price effect vanishes.

## Example 10.8: Fertility Equation with a Trend

```{julia}
#=
use "data/fertil3.dta", clear
reg gfr pe ww2 pill t

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
         gfr | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
          pe |   .2788778   .0400199     6.97   0.000     .1989978    .3587578
         ww2 |  -35.59228   6.297377    -5.65   0.000     -48.1619   -23.02266
        pill |   .9974479    6.26163     0.16   0.874    -11.50082    13.49571
           t |  -1.149872   .1879038    -6.12   0.000    -1.524929   -.7748145
       _cons |   111.7694   3.357765    33.29   0.000     105.0673    118.4716
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Number of obs = 72,  F(4,67) = 32.84,  R-squared = 0.6622,  Root MSE = 11.849
=#
m108a = stata_regress(fertil3; y = :gfr, x = [:pe, :ww2, :pill, :t])
```

```{julia}
#=
reg gfr pe ww2 pill t tsq

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
         gfr | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
          pe |   .3478126   .0402599     8.64   0.000     .2674311     .428194
         ww2 |  -35.88028   5.707921    -6.29   0.000    -47.27651   -24.48404
        pill |  -10.11972   6.336094    -1.60   0.115    -22.77014    2.530696
           t |  -2.531426   .3893863    -6.50   0.000    -3.308861   -1.753991
         tsq |   .0196126    .004971     3.95   0.000     .0096876    .0295377
       _cons |   124.0919   4.360738    28.46   0.000     115.3854    132.7984
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Number of obs = 72,  F(5,66) = 35.09,  R-squared = 0.7267,  Root MSE = 10.739
=#
m108b = stata_regress(fertil3; y = :gfr, x = [:pe, :ww2, :pill, :t, :tsq])
```

**Exact match** (N = 72; `tsq` is the squared trend, already stored).

## Example 10.9: Puerto Rican Employment with a Trend

```{julia}
#=
use "data/prminwge.dta", clear
reg lprepop lmincov lusgnp t

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
     lprepop | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
     lmincov |  -.1686949   .0442463    -3.81   0.001    -.2586142   -.0787757
      lusgnp |   1.057351   .1766369     5.99   0.000     .6983813     1.41632
           t |  -.0323542   .0050227    -6.44   0.000    -.0425616   -.0221468
       _cons |  -8.696298   1.295764    -6.71   0.000    -11.32961   -6.062988
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Number of obs = 38,  F(3,34) = 62.78,  R-squared = 0.8471,  Root MSE = .03793
=#
m109a = stata_regress(prminwge; y = :lprepop, x = [:lmincov, :lusgnp, :t])
```

The no-trend regression from Example 10.3, for comparison:

```{julia}
#=
reg lprepop lmincov lusgnp   (identical to Example 10.3: N = 38, R-squared = 0.6605)
=#
m109b = stata_regress(prminwge; y = :lprepop, x = [:lmincov, :lusgnp])
```

**Exact match.** Adding the trend flips the `lusgnp` coefficient from small and
insignificant to large and significant.

## Example 10.10: Housing Investment — Detrending

```{julia}
#=
use "data/hseinv.dta", clear
reg linvpc t

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      linvpc | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
           t |   .0081459   .0018129     4.49   0.000     .0044819    .0118098
       _cons |  -.8412918    .044744   -18.80   0.000    -.9317228   -.7508608
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Number of obs = 42,  F(1,40) = 20.19,  R-squared = 0.3354,  Root MSE = .14241
=#
m1010a = stata_regress(hseinv; y = :linvpc, x = [:t])
```

Detrend `linvpc` (take the residuals `linvpch` from the regression on `t`),
then regress the detrended series on `lprice` and `t`. This reproduces the
partialling-out logic: the `lprice` slope and its SE match the full regression,
while the trend coefficient shrinks to the part of `t` orthogonal to the
detrended response.

```{julia}
#=
predict linvpch, res
reg linvpch lprice t

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
     linvpch | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
      lprice |  -.3809612   .6788352    -0.56   0.578    -1.754035    .9921126
           t |   .0016828   .0035122     0.48   0.635    -.0054213    .0087869
       _cons |  -.0717677   .1356133    -0.53   0.600    -.3460716    .2025361
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Number of obs = 42,  F(2,39) = 0.16,  R-squared = 0.0080,  Root MSE = .14364
=#
df1010 = copy(hseinv)
df1010.linvpch = StatsBase.residuals(m1010a, df1010)   # residuals from reg linvpc t
m1010b = stata_regress(df1010; y = :linvpch, x = [:lprice, :t])
```

```{julia}
#=
reg linvpc lprice t   (Example 10.7 again: lprice = -.3809612, SE .6788352)
=#
m1010c = stata_regress(hseinv; y = :linvpc, x = [:lprice, :t])
```

**Exact match.** The `lprice` coefficient (−.3809612) and SE (.6788352) are
identical in the detrended regression and the full regression, illustrating the
Frisch–Waugh–Lovell result.

## Example 10.11: Antidumping Filings with Seasonal Dummies

```{julia}
#=
use "data/barium.dta", clear
reg lchnimp lchempi lgas lrtwex befile6 affile6 afdec6 feb mar apr may jun jul aug sep oct nov dec

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
     lchnimp | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95% conf. interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
     lchempi |    3.26506   .4929302     6.62   0.000     2.288476    4.241644
        lgas |  -1.278121   1.389008    -0.92   0.359    -4.029997    1.473755
      lrtwex |   .6630496   .4713038     1.41   0.162    -.2706882    1.596787
     befile6 |   .1397024   .2668075     0.52   0.602    -.3888914    .6682962
     affile6 |   .0126317   .2786866     0.05   0.964    -.5394967    .5647601
      afdec6 |  -.5213006     .30195    -1.73   0.087    -1.119518    .0769168
         feb |  -.4177089   .3044445    -1.37   0.173    -1.020868    .1854505
         mar |   .0590528   .2647308     0.22   0.824    -.4654266    .5835322
         apr |  -.4514825   .2683865    -1.68   0.095    -.9832046    .0802396
         may |   .0333085   .2692425     0.12   0.902    -.5001093    .5667264
         jun |  -.2063321   .2692515    -0.77   0.445    -.7397679    .3271038
         jul |   .0038354   .2787665     0.01   0.989    -.5484513    .5561222
         aug |  -.1570652   .2779928    -0.56   0.573    -.7078191    .3936887
         sep |  -.1341606   .2676556    -0.50   0.617    -.6644348    .3961135
         oct |   .0516921   .2668512     0.19   0.847    -.4769883    .5803725
         nov |    -.24626   .2628272    -0.94   0.351     -.766968     .274448
         dec |   .1328368   .2714234     0.49   0.626    -.4049019    .6705755
       _cons |   16.77877   32.42865     0.52   0.606    -47.46824    81.02577
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Number of obs = 131,  F(17,113) = 3.71,  R-squared = 0.3583,  Root MSE = .60121
=#
m1011 = stata_regress(barium; y = :lchnimp,
    x = [:lchempi, :lgas, :lrtwex, :befile6, :affile6, :afdec6,
         :feb, :mar, :apr, :may, :jun, :jul, :aug, :sep, :oct, :nov, :dec])
```

```{julia}
#=
test feb mar apr may jun jul aug sep oct nov dec
       F( 11,   113) =    0.86
            Prob > F =    0.5852
=#
stata_test(m1011, ["feb", "mar", "apr", "may", "jun", "jul", "aug", "sep",
                   "oct", "nov", "dec"])
```

**Exact match.** The joint test of the eleven monthly dummies (F = 0.86,
p = 0.5852) shows no evidence of seasonality once the other regressors are
included.

# Verification summary — Chapter 10

| Example | Kind | Stata | Julia | Match |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10.1 `reg inf unem` | OLS | unem .5023782, N=56 | unem .4676257, N=49 | **No — data vintage** |
| 10.2 `reg i3 inf def` | OLS | inf .6058659, N=56 | same | yes |
| 10.3 `reg lprepop lmincov lusgnp` | OLS | lmincov −.1544442 | same | yes |
| 10.4 `reg gfr pe ww2 pill` | OLS | pe .08254 | same | yes |
| 10.4 `reg gfr … pe_1 pe_2` | OLS | R²=0.4986, N=70 | same | yes |
| 10.4 `test pe_1 pe_2` | F-test | F=0.05, p=0.9480 | same | yes |
| 10.4 LRP | scalar | .10071909 | .10071909 | yes |
| 10.4 reparam `pe` (=LRP) | OLS | .1007191 (.0298027) | same | yes |
| 10.5 `reg lchnimp …` | OLS | afdec6 −.565245 | same | yes |
| 10.5 %Δ barium | scalar | −43.177908 | −43.177908 | yes |
| 10.6 `reg demvote … if year<1996` | OLS | incum .0543902, N=20 | same | yes |
| 10.6 predicted demvote | scalar | .5012655 | .5012655 | yes |
| 10.7 `reg linvpc lprice` | OLS | lprice 1.240943 | same | yes |
| 10.7 `reg linvpc lprice t` | OLS | t .0098287 | same | yes |
| 10.8 `reg gfr pe ww2 pill t` | OLS | pe .2788778 | same | yes |
| 10.8 `+ tsq` | OLS | tsq .0196126 | same | yes |
| 10.9 `reg lprepop … t` | OLS | lusgnp 1.057351 | same | yes |
| 10.10 `reg linvpc t` | OLS | t .0081459 | same | yes |
| 10.10 detrended `reg linvpch lprice t` | OLS | lprice −.3809612 | same | yes |
| 10.11 `reg lchnimp … + months` | OLS | lchempi 3.26506, N=131 | same | yes |
| 10.11 `test` months | F-test | F=0.86, p=0.5852 | same | yes |

All examples are **plain OLS** (`stata_regress`) plus a few scalar displays and one
residual/detrending step, all reproduced exactly — **except Example 10.1**, where
the bundled `phillips.dta` is an earlier vintage (49 obs, 1948–1996) than the one
behind the published output (56 obs, 1948–2003). The discrepancy is a data
difference, not an estimation difference.
